verses.commentary: John opens with Genesis 1:1 echoes. The Word (Logos, λόγος) was eternally with God and was God. Jesus is not created. He is the eternal second person of the Trinity, the divine agent of creation and revelation.
Eternal preexistence affirmed. Before creation, the Word existed in relationship with the Father. The Trinity exists in eternal communion.
All creation comes through the Word. Nothing exists apart from Him. Jesus is the cosmic creator, not merely a teacher or prophet. Colossians 1:16 expands this truth.
Life and light originate in Christ. He is the source of all biological and spiritual life. Light represents revelation, truth, and salvation. Darkness cannot comprehend or overcome it.
The light shines in darkness. Present tense: continuous action. The darkness did not overcome (katalambano, καταλαμβάνω) it: neither comprehend nor extinguish. Evil cannot defeat Christ.
John the Baptist enters. Sent from God as witness. His purpose: point to the light, not be the light. True ministry deflects attention to Christ.
Witness (martyria, μαρτυρία) means testimony given at cost. John testifies so all might believe. Faith comes through the witness of those who have seen Christ.
John explicitly denies being the light. He knows his role. Humility and clarity mark true servants of God. They point beyond themselves.
The true light gives light to everyone. Universal scope: Christ enlightens all humanity with general revelation and offers salvation to all who believe.
Tragic irony. The Creator entered His creation, yet the world did not recognize Him. Spiritual blindness prevents recognition of the obvious.
He came to His own (Israel, the covenant people), and they rejected Him. The deepest tragedy: those who should have known Him best refused Him.
Yet some received Him. To receive is to believe in His name. The result: authority (exousia, ἐξουσία) to become children of God. Not natural birth, but spiritual adoption.
Divine birth, not human effort. Not natural descent, not human decision, not human will. Salvation is entirely God's work. The new birth is from above (John 3:3).
The incarnation. The Word became flesh (sarx, σάρξ). God took on full humanity. He tabernacled (eskēnōsen, ἐσκήνωσεν) among us, echoing the Exodus tabernacle. Glory, grace, and truth dwell in Jesus.
John the Baptist's testimony continues. Jesus surpasses John because He existed before John. Eternal preexistence again affirmed. The lesser points to the greater.
From His fullness (plērōma, πλήρωμα) we receive grace upon grace. Continuous, abundant supply. One wave of grace follows another. Christ is the inexhaustible source.
Law came through Moses; grace and truth through Jesus Christ. Not opposition but fulfillment. The Law reveals sin; grace provides salvation. Christ is superior to Moses.
No one has seen God. The Father is invisible. But the Son, who is Himself God and dwells in the Father's bosom, has revealed (exēgēsato, ἐξηγήσατο) Him. Jesus is the exegesis of God.
Religious authorities interrogate John. Identity questions drive the narrative. Who is John? The question will shift: Who is Jesus?
John confesses freely: I am not the Messiah. Clear, emphatic denial. He knows who he is not. Humility precedes exaltation of Christ.
Are you Elijah? No. The Prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15)? No. John denies messianic or prophetic identity beyond his specific role as forerunner.
Persistent questioning. Who are you? They need an answer. Self-definition becomes necessary when others demand it.
John quotes Isaiah 40:3. A voice in the wilderness. Make straight the way for the Lord. John is a voice, not the Word. He prepares hearts for Jesus.
Pharisees sent the delegation. Religious authorities investigate anyone claiming prophetic authority. Control and orthodoxy drive their concerns.
Why baptize if you are not the Messiah, Elijah, or the Prophet? Valid question. John's authority is questioned because his actions imply eschatological significance.
John baptizes with water, a preparatory act. But among you stands one you do not know. Jesus is already present, unrecognized. Divine presence often goes unseen.
John is unworthy to untie Jesus' sandal. A slave's task. Radical humility from the greatest prophet. Jesus infinitely surpasses John.
Bethany beyond the Jordan, the location. Geography matters in John's Gospel. This is where Israel crossed into the Promised Land under Joshua. New exodus begins.
Behold, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. Sacrificial language. Passover lamb, Isaiah 53 servant, scapegoat. Jesus is the ultimate sin bearer.
Jesus ranks above John because He existed before John. Preexistence again. The chronological paradox reveals eternal identity.
John did not know Jesus initially. But through baptism and revelation, Jesus is revealed to Israel. John's mission: make the Messiah manifest.
John testifies: I saw the Spirit descend like a dove and remain on Him. The Spirit's descent confirms Jesus' identity. The anointing of the Messiah.
God told John: The one on whom the Spirit descends is the one who baptizes with the Holy Spirit. Sign given in advance. Jesus brings the eschatological Spirit.
John's conclusion: I have seen and testified that this is the Son of God. Eyewitness testimony. Jesus is divinely anointed, divine Son, God's chosen one.
Next day, John with two disciples. Transition from John's ministry to Jesus' ministry. Disciples will shift allegiance from the forerunner to the Messiah.
John repeats: Behold, the Lamb of God. Repeated witness reinforces truth. The lamb imagery is central to understanding Jesus' mission.
The two disciples follow Jesus. John's witness produces faith. True witness leads people away from the witness to Christ.
Jesus asks: What are you seeking? First words of Jesus in John's Gospel. The question penetrates motive and desire. They call Him Rabbi (teacher). They want to learn.
Come and see. Invitation to experience. They came, saw where He stayed, and remained with Him. Abiding (menō, μένω) is a key Johannine theme. Faith leads to abiding.
Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, was one of the two. Named disciple. His encounter with Jesus changes everything. He will bring others to Jesus.
Andrew finds his brother Simon and declares: We have found the Messiah (Christos, Χριστός). First evangelism in John. Personal encounter leads to personal proclamation.
Jesus renames Simon: You are Cephas (Peter, rock). Jesus sees Peter's future identity, not present condition. Divine renaming signifies transformation and calling.
Jesus decides to go to Galilee. He finds Philip and calls him: Follow me. Divine initiative. Jesus seeks and calls. Discipleship begins with Christ's summons.
Philip from Bethsaida, same town as Andrew and Peter. Geography connects the disciples. Shared origins, shared mission.
Philip finds Nathanael and proclaims: We have found Him of whom Moses and the prophets wrote. Jesus is the fulfillment of Old Testament expectation. Scripture points to Christ.
Can anything good come from Nazareth? Nathanael's skepticism. Regional prejudice. Yet he is willing to investigate: Come and see. Honest doubt yields to truth.
Jesus sees Nathanael and declares: A true Israelite in whom there is no deceit. Jacob was a deceiver; Nathanael is guileless. Jesus sees the heart.
How do you know me? Jesus replies: I saw you under the fig tree. Divine omniscience. Jesus knows what human eyes cannot see. Nathanael is stunned.
Nathanael's confession: Rabbi, You are the Son of God! You are the King of Israel! Full messianic confession. Belief comes through encounter with Christ's knowledge and presence.
Jesus promises greater things. Belief based on a sign is the beginning, not the end. Deeper revelation awaits those who follow.
You will see heaven opened and angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man. Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12). Jesus is the connection between heaven and earth. He is the true mediator.
